verses.commentary: Therefore: in light of Christ's suffering (3:18), arm yourselves (hoplizō, ὁπλίζω) with the same attitude. Military imagery: put on the mindset of suffering. Whoever suffers in the body is done with sin. Suffering for righteousness severs ties with sinful patterns. Physical suffering for Christ purifies motives and desires.

As a result, they live not for human desires but for God's will. Reorientation of life purpose. Before: driven by fleshly cravings. After: governed by divine intent. Suffering shifts priorities from self to God.

You have spent enough time doing what pagans do: debauchery (aselgeia, ἀσέλγεια), lust, drunkenness, orgies, carousing, detestable idolatry. Catalog of former life. Enough time wasted on sin. The past is sufficient for evil; the present is for righteousness.

Pagans are surprised (xenizō, ξενίζω): think it strange, astonished. Believers no longer join reckless, wild living. Conversion changes behavior, and the world notices. They heap abuse on you: ridicule, insult. Righteous living provokes hostility from those still in darkness.

They will give account to Him who is ready to judge the living and the dead. God is prepared to judge. No one escapes accountability. The wicked mock now but will answer later. Final judgment vindicates the righteous and condemns the rebellious.

The gospel was preached even to those now dead. Purpose: they might be judged according to human standards in the body but live according to God in the spirit. Death is not the final word. The gospel reaches even those who die, offering spiritual life beyond physical death.

The end of all things is near. Eschatological urgency: history is moving toward consummation. Be alert (sōphroneō, σωφρονέω): clear-minded, self-controlled. Be of sober mind for prayer (proseuchē, προσευχή). Imminence of Christ's return motivates vigilance and devotion.

Above all, love each other deeply (ektenos, ἐκτενῶς): fervently, earnestly. Love covers a multitude of sins (Proverbs 10:12). Not hiding sin but forgiving it. Love overlooks offenses, extends grace, and restores relationships. Priority of love in the Christian community.

Offer hospitality (philoxenos, φιλόξενος): love of strangers. Without grumbling (gongysmos, γογγυσμός): complaining, murmuring. Hospitality should be joyful, not burdensome. Welcoming others reflects God's welcome of sinners.

Each has received a gift (charisma, χάρισμα): grace gift from God. Use it to serve others as faithful stewards (oikonomos, οἰκονόμος) of God's grace in its various forms (poikilos, ποικίλος): manifold, diverse. Gifts are for the body, not for self. Stewardship requires faithfulness, not perfection.

If anyone speaks, do so as one who speaks God's very words. If anyone serves, do so with the strength God provides. Purpose: in all things God may be praised through Jesus Christ. All ministry is empowered by God and directed to His glory. Human effort without divine strength is futile. To Him be glory and power forever. Amen.

Do not be surprised (xenizō, ξενίζω) at the fiery ordeal (pyrōsis, πύρωσις): burning, trial by fire. Testing is normal, not strange. Something strange would be a life without trials. Suffering refines, tests, and purifies faith. Expect persecution, not comfort.

Rejoice inasmuch as you participate (koinōneō, κοινωνέω) in Christ's sufferings. Sharing His sufferings now leads to sharing His glory later. You will be overjoyed (agalliaō, ἀγαλλιάω) when His glory is revealed. Present suffering, future joy. Participation in Christ includes both.

If insulted because of the name of Christ, you are blessed (makarios, μακάριος). The Spirit of glory and of God rests on you. Persecution for Christ's name brings divine presence. The Spirit glorifies God in suffering saints. Insult becomes honor; rejection becomes blessing.

If you suffer, it should not be as a murderer, thief, criminal, or meddler (allotriepiskopos, ἀλλοτριεπίσκοπος): busybody, one who interferes in others' affairs. Suffering for sin brings no honor. Righteous suffering, not self-inflicted consequences, glorifies God.

If you suffer as a Christian (Christianos, Χριστιανός), do not be ashamed. Praise God that you bear that name. The name Christian was initially a term of reproach (Acts 11:26). Peter reclaims it as a badge of honor. Suffering for Christ's name is privilege, not disgrace.

It is time for judgment to begin with God's household. Purifying judgment starts with believers, not unbelievers. If it begins with us, what will the outcome be for those who do not obey the gospel? If the righteous face trials, the wicked face wrath. Present discipline versus future condemnation.

Proverbs 11:31 quoted: If it is hard for the righteous to be saved, what will become of the ungodly and sinner? Salvation is costly, requiring God's Son. If believers endure trials, unbelievers face eternal judgment. The difficulty of salvation highlights the severity of rejecting it.

Those who suffer according to God's will should commit (paratithēmi, παρατίθημι) themselves to their faithful Creator. Continue to do good. Trust God's character in suffering. Faithfulness in trials honors the faithful Creator. Suffering is not random; it is under divine sovereignty.
